title: High-Efficiency Broadband Mid-Infrared Absorption in Asymmetrically Matched Metallic Meanders: Development of Ti40V60 Alloy based LEKIDs for Mid-Far IR
authors: Shilpam Sharma, Shekhar Chandra Pandey, Anudeep Singh, Shankar Lal, R. S. Saini, M. K. Chattopadhyay
abstract: Fast, highly sensitive, and broadband detectors operating in the mid-to-far-infrared (MIR-FIR) spectral region are essential for applications ranging from astrophysics to time-resolved spectroscopy using laboratory-based sources and beamlines at Infrared Free-Electron Laser (IR-FEL) facilities. Conventional Lumped Element Kinetic Inductance Detectors (LEKID) achieve high optical efficiency using resonant quarter wavelength (λ/4) backshort cavities. However, this cavity-based approach is inherently narrowband and becomes optomechanically challenging at the mid to far infrared (MIR-FIR) wavelengths. Here, we present a completely backshort-free LEKID absorber architecture that achieves broadband absorption exceeding 50%. The meander absorbers were fabricated from a superconducting Ti-V alloy and characterized using IR radiation from the IR-FEL at RRCAT, India. By illuminating through the silicon substrate and matching the meander's sheet resistance to the silicon substrate's wave impedance, the front-side reflection is strongly suppressed. Simultaneously, the sub-wavelength periodicity of the dense meander inhibits propagating transmission into free space via evanescent wave confinement. This combined mechanism drives efficient Ohmic dissipation within the metallic meander, yielding an experimental absorption efficiency ranging from 50% to 90% across the 12.5-30 micrometer wavelength range. The cavity-free design greatly simplifies the fabrication of the focal plane array while providing the broadband response required for next-generation detectors. To assess the suitability of the Ti40V60 alloy as an active superconducting detector material, a test LEKID resonator was also designed, fabricated, and experimentally characterized.
summary: This work introduces a breakthrough detector design for mid-to-far infrared spectroscopy: a cavity-free absorber built from superconducting metallic meanders. By engineering the structure's impedance and exploiting sub-wavelength periodicity, the authors achieve high broadband absorption without the complexity of traditional resonant cavities. This establishes a scalable platform for next-generation MIR-FIR quantum detectors.
main_problem: The primary challenge is developing fast, highly sensitive, and broadband detectors for the mid-to-far-infrared (MIR-FIR) spectral region.
main_result: A novel, cavity-free LEKID absorber architecture using superconducting Ti-V alloy meanders achieves high broadband absorption (50-90%) across 12.5-30 $\mu$m, simplifying detector fabrication significantly.
method: The authors combine electromagnetic simulation (CST) with experimental characterization using an IR Free-Electron Laser (IR-FEL) to measure optical transmission and reflection.
model_or_system: The system involves patterned metallic meander absorbers ($	ext{Ti}_{40}	ext{V}_{60}$) deposited on a silicon substrate. The mechanism relies on impedance matching at the front side and evanescent wave confinement due to sub-wavelength periodicity.
key_observables: Absorption efficiency (50-90%), broadband spectral response ($12.5 - 30 \mu	ext{m}$), and microwave resonance dip in a test LEKID resonator.
important_parameters: Wavelength range ($\lambda$), meander line spacing ($	ext{LS} = 3, 6, 12 \mu	ext{m}$), superconducting material ($	ext{Ti}_{40}	ext{V}_{60}$), and wave impedance matching ($Z_{	ext{meander}} \sim 110 \Omega$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that substrate effects can be mathematically de-embedded from measured optical data, and for the microwave test, it compares simulated vs. measured resonance behavior.
figures_summary: Figures show schematic meander structures, experimental setups using IR-FEL, simulation models of the LEKID resonator, and spectral plots demonstrating high absorption enhancement as line spacing decreases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the limitations of conventional narrowband cavity-based detectors to proposing a novel, broadband, cavity-free absorber architecture. It details the physics (impedance matching + confinement) through simulation/theory, validates it with optical measurements across a wide spectral range, and finally confirms superconducting functionality via microwave characterization.
why_interesting: While primarily an applied condensed matter/detector physics paper, the concept of using metamaterial periodicity to engineer near-perfect absorption by coupling incident energy into confined Ohmic losses is relevant to understanding loss mechanisms in quantum optical cavities or engineered dissipation channels in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 (a) Zoomed-in image of the schematic meander structure illustrating the meander. p, w, and s represent the pitch, width, and separation of the meander lines. (b) Schematic diagram of the meander structure on the substrate used for CST simulation. The left and right boundaries (green) were assigned as perfect electric conductor (PEC) boundaries, while the upper and lower boundaries (blue) were defined as perfect magnetic conductor (PMC) boundaries. The ports normal to the absorber plane (purple) were set as open boundaries for excitation of the IR radiation along the meander lines.

Fig. 2 Photographs of the experimental optical measurement setup used for simultaneous transmission and reflection characterization of the fabricated meander absorber structures using the IR-FEL (right side). Red arrows represent the direction and path of the IR-FEL beam. The setup includes beam steering mirrors, focusing optics, pyroelectric detectors, and a home-built Michelson interferometer (left side) for independent wavelength determination.

Fig. 3 (a) CST simulation model of the LEKID resonator showing the electromagnetic enclosure, applied boundary conditions, and excitation through the multi-pin waveguide port. (b) Detailed view of the LEKID geometry illustrating the microstrip feedline, meandered inductor, interdigitated capacitor, silicon substrate, and ground plane.
Fig. 3(a). A detailed view of the resonator geometry is presented in Fig. 3(b). The side walls
